coredata.dc:title: Vancomycin-modified nitrogen and chloride doped carbon dots and their application as a Staphylococcus aureus probe
coredata.dc:creator: Tabaraki, Reza
Nazari, Fereshteh
coredata.dc:description: In this research, N, Cl-doped carbon dots (N, Cl-CDs) were prepared in choline chloride-glycerol deep eutectic solvent (DES) by microwave method. N, Cl-CDs surface was modified with vancomycin for detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) bacteria in the range of 102-107 colony-forming unit per milliliter (CFU/mL). The detection limit was 101 CFU/mL. Morphology and structure of N, Cl-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) and zeta potential. The prepared N, Cl-CDs had excellent dispersion in water, particle size range of 2–3 nm, and quantum yield of 38.75%. Speed, wide linear range and more convenient were advantages of new probe with respect to other methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Deep eutectic solvent
Doped carbon dots
Microwave
Fluorescent probe
Vancomycin
Staphylococcus aureus

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs modified with vancomycin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the CDs, and (B) absorption, excitation and fluorescence spectra.
FT-IR spectra of A) CDs, B) vancomycin, and C) CDs@vancomycin.
(A) and EDXS image of N, Cl-CDs (B) zeta potential.
XPS spectra of the N, Cl-CDs (A), high resolution C 1s (B), O 1s (C), N 1s (D), and Cl 2p peaks (E).
Relative fluorescence intensity of (A) CDs@van, (B) N, Cl-CDs, and (C) N, Cl-CDs@van in the presence of various bacteria.

(A) Effect of incubation time on fluorescence of the N, Cl-CDs@van/S. aureus system; concentration of S. aureus: 104 CFU ml− 1 (B) Fluorescence spectra of N, Cl-CDs@van in the presence of different concentrations of S. aureus, (C) Stern-Volmer plot at various concentration of (102, 103, 104, 105, 106 and 107 CFU ml− 1) with increasing S. aureus concentration, (D) Fluorescence spectra of N, Cl-CDs (without vancomycine) in the presence of different concentration of (104, 105, 106 and 107 CFU ml− 1) of S. aureus.
Comparison of different methods for S. aureus determination.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MB: magnetic bead; NP: nano-particles; NC: nano-clusters; Van: vancomycin; Apt: aptamer; QDs: quantum dots; CDs: carbon dots; IgG: Immunoglobulin G; APBA: 3-aminophenylboronic acid; BON: breakable organosilica nanocapsule.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of S. aureus in orange juice by the proposed method (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Vancomycin-modified nitrogen and chloride doped carbon dots and their application as a Staphylococcus aureus probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, N, Cl-doped carbon dots (N, Cl-CDs) were prepared in choline chloride-glycerol deep eutectic solvent (DES) by microwave method. N, Cl-CDs surface was modified with vancomycin for detection of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) bacteria in the range of 102-107 colony-forming unit per milliliter (CFU/mL). The detection limit was 101 CFU/mL. Morphology and structure of N, Cl-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS), photoluminescence spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS) and zeta potential. The prepared N, Cl-CDs had excellent dispersion in water, particle size range of 2–3 nm, and quantum yield of 38.75%. Speed, wide linear range and more convenient were advantages of new probe with respect to other methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Human health is threatened by pathogenic infections. Staphylococcus aureus is an anaerobic and Gram-positive bacterium (round shape, size = 1 μm, discovered 1880) [1]. S. aureus found in the nose, on skin and respiratory tract and causing meningitis, boil skin, urinary infections and pneumonia. S. aureus produces at least eight enterotoxins that damage the biological membranes and cause the cell death. S. aureus is one at the top of the five most important pathogens of foodborne illnesses [2].

Different methods were used for S. aureus detection. Examples of immune-based assays were enzyme linked immunosensor assay (ELISA) [3], quartz crystal microbalance [4], long-period fiber grating (LPFG) sensor [5], biolayer interferometry (BLI) [6] and microfluidic devices and impedance spectroscopy [7]. Different analytical techniques were used in aptamer-based assays such as chronoamperometry [8], square wave voltammetry and colorimetry [9], electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [10], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [11,12], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [13] and fluorescence [14]. The metal-organic framework (MOF) [15], magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (MMIP) [16] and magnetic nano-particles [17–21] were also used for the separation and detection of Staphylococcus aureus.

Vancomycin is a glycopeptide antibiotic that binds to the peptidoglycan precursors in the cell wall of gram-positive bacteria such as S. aureus. A stable and noncovalent complex forms between the vancomycin and the peptidoglycan. This complex inhibits late-stage of the peptidoglycan biosynthesis in the cell wall [22]. Based on this property, different vancomycin-based strategies were developed for S. aureus detection [23–32]. Some of these methods need to high cost recognition element (aptamer and immune compounds) with confined resources, complicated and several preparation steps, skilled personnel, and expensive and complex instruments. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop a rapid, simple, low cost and sensitive detection method.

Fluorescent carbon dots with stable and tunable photoluminescence, small size and bio-compatibility have attracted considerable attention in bio-imaging, biological labeling, photo-catalyst and rapidly identification of bacterial pathogen [33]. Application of the CDs for bacteria detection requires surface charge and functional groups modification. The surface-passivation reagents are usually used for this purpose. The CDs without surface passivation had low quantum yields. Fluorescent characteristics of the CDs depend on precursors, solvent, and passivation agent [34]. CDs were also used for S. aureus detection [35,36].

Among different precursors including water, diethylene glycol, deep eutectic solvent (DES) and ionic liquids, DES have been established as useful media for the synthesis of a wide variety carbon-based nanomaterials [37]. For DES preparation, a hydrogen bond donor (HBD) is mixed with a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) in a certain molar ratio and temperature. In the last decade, interest in deep eutectic solvent has increased rapidly because DESs show many important unique physicochemical properties such as high polarity, low volatility, diversity of precursors for its design, non-flammability, low cost, low toxicity and biodegradability. In addition to solvent role of the DESs, they are dopant because they contain abundant elements. Other advantages of these solvents are time and cost-effectiveness and reduction of chemicals consumption [38].

In this study, a single-step approach was used to microwave-assisted synthesis of N, Cl-CDs in glycerol and choline chloride solvent (DES). DES served not only as solvent but also as N and Cl doping agent that reduced consumption of chemicals. These CDs were modified by vancomycin (N, Cl-CDs@van) which is widely available, stable and much cheaper than other common used targeting groups (e.g. antibody and aptamer) for detection of Gram-positive bacteria. Then, N, Cl-CDs@van was used to detect Staphylococcus aureus based on the decrease of N, Cl-CDs fluorescence. The ligand–receptor interactions are reason of the binding of vancomycin to the surfaces of bacteria. In contrast with other methods, N, Cl-CDs exhibited good selectivity for Staphylococcus aureus, simple preparation procedure, a wide linearity range, and acceptable detection limit.

Glycerol, vancomycin, choline chloride, glucose and phosphate buffer (0.1 M) were analytical grade (Merck). Studied bacteria were Staphylococcus aureus, Bacilli, Escherichia coli, Klebsiella pneumonia, Streptococcus agalactiae and Salmonella enteritidis (Department of microbiology, Ilam University, Iran).

X-ray photon spectroscopy (BESTEC, EA 10), Varian Cary 300 Bio, MC30 Philips, X-ray spectroscopy (MIRA II TESCAN), Vertex 70 FT-IR, TEM (MCM120), and Zeta potential (Horiba, SZ100) were used for XPS, UV–Vis, Fluorescence, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDXS), FT-IR, TEM and Zeta potential respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs modified with vancomycin
ce:section.ce:para: Deep eutectic solvent was synthesized by a mixing of 1.52 g choline chloride and 4 mL of glycerol (molar ratio of 1:2). They were heated at 100 °C with constant stirring until a homogeneous liquid appeared. Then, 0.1 g of glucose was dissolved in 6 mL of this solvent and heated in a microwave oven (350 W, 3 min). The final brown residue was added to 50 mL phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4), centrifuged (12000 rpm for 20 min) and filtered (0.4 μm). Finally, vancomycin solution (20 mg in 2 mL phosphate buffer, 20 mM, pH = 7.4) was gently mixed to 3 mL CDs solution and sonicated for 2 h [30].

Gram-positive bacteria (Streptococcus agalactiae, Bacilli, and Staphylococcus aureus) and Gram-negative bacteria (Klebsiella pneumonia, Salmonella enteritidis and Escherichia coli) were cultured (Luria Bertani broth, 37 °C, 24 h). Media concentration was 0.5 McFarland. Different dilutions (102, 103, 104, 105, 106, and 107) were made using phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4). Then, 100 μL of N, Cl-CDs@van solution and 100 μL (107 CFU/mL) solution were added to 3 mL buffer phosphate. This system was used as a turn off probe for Staphylococcus aureus. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 540 nm (λex = 430 nm) G+

The fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) was determined by a relative optical method [39]. Quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4, ΦR = 0.54) was used as reference. The reference and CDs absorbance were measured at λex = 320 nm. The CDs and reference solutions were diluted until their absorbances were <0.1 at λex [39]. The fluorescence spectra (370–600 nm) of the reference and CDs were also recorded when λex = 320 nm. The quantum yield was calculated as:
Φ X

λ 2

A R

)
1 −
10
−
A X
(
λ
e x
)
×
η X 2
η R 2
where subscripts X and R denote sample and reference, η is the refractive index of solvent. The second term is spectrally integrated photon flux at the detector (the area under the corrected emission spectrum I c(λ ex, λ em)). A is absorbance (the third term in above equation is the fraction of the excitation light absorbed).

Size of the synthesized carbon dots was 2–3 nm with regular in shape and good uniformity (Fig. 1 A). Fig. 1B shows the maximum emission was located at 547 nm when excitation was at 430 nm. As shown in Fig. 1B, the absorption band centered on 280 nm was assigned for the π→π* transitions for aromatic sp2 hybridization [40]. The FT-IR was used to identify functional groups on the N, Cl-CDs, Vancomycin and N, Cl-CDs@Van. Based on FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 ), stretching vibrations bands of O–H (3400 cm−1), CO (1640 cm−1), CN (3400 cm−1), C–O (1480 cm−1), CC (1070 cm−1) and C–Cl (700 cm−1) were assigned for N, Cl-CDs, vancomycin and N, Cl-CDs @van [30]. Shift in CO frequency from 1645 to 1713 cm−1 (in the FT-IR spectra of vancomycin and N, Cl-CDs@van) was attributed to binding of the vancomycin to CD surface [31]. Appearance of more bands in the N, Cl-CDs@van FT-IR spectrum arises from this attachment. As shown in the EDXS spectrum (Fig. 3 A), the synthesized N, Cl-CDs are composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and chloride that proves N and Cl doping [40]. Based on the zeta potential results (Fig. 3B), the N, Cl-CDs have negative zeta potential with a value of −10.8 mV at neutral pH which may be attributed to the amino, hydroxyl and carboxylic groups present on the N, Cl-CDs [28].

The XPS results are shown in Fig. 4 . Four dominant peaks located at 284.50, 399.64, 532.73 and 197.76 eV were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Cl 2p, respectively. Fig. 4B shows the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s for the N, Cl-CDs and peaks at 284.39, 285.89, 288.38 and 291.40 eV were assigned to carbon in the CC/C–C, C–OH/C–N, COOH and π→π* shake-up transition, respectively [41]. The C–OH/C–O–C (531.45 eV), CO (532.31 eV) and physically adsorbed O2 on the surface (533.37 eV) [42] were also assigned in high resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4C). In N 1s high resolution spectrum (Fig. 4D), peaks at 399.79, 400.55 and 402.09 eV were assigned to amine/amide N, pyrrolic N and quaternized nitrogen (–N + Me3)/N-oxides, respectively [43–45]. As shown in Fig. 4E, the Cl 2p had two peaks around 198.27 and 199.84 eV which are the binding energies of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 due to the spin-orbit splitting of the Cl 2p core level (1.6 eV) [46,47].

The batch-to-batch RSD was measured by performing repeated synthesis (n = 3) on the same conditions. RSD in carbon dots fluorescence was 6.8%. The synthesized N, Cl-CDs had high quantum yield (38.75%). The doping of nitrogen and chloride atoms in the CDs could introduce new surface state, and thus enhances the fluorescence performance.

Effect of the Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella enteritidis, Klebsiella pneumonia, Bacilli, Streptococcus agalactiae and Escherichia coli on the fluorescence of different CDs was tested (Fig. 5 ). In Fig. 5A, carbon dots were prepared from glucose in water (without N and Cl doping) and then modified with van (CDs@van). In Fig. 5C, CDs were doped with N and Cl elements and then modified with van (N, Cl-CDs@van). As shown, selectivity for the Staphylococcus aureus was improved. In Fig. 5B, N, Cl-CDs were not modified by van. As shown, interaction between CDs and bacteria was very weak. Effect of the presence of other bacteria on the Staphylococcus aureus measurement (concentration: 102 CFU ml−1) was also investigated. The tolerance limits were determined for a maximum error of 5% in fluorescence intensity. The tolerable concentration ratios (in the parentheses) for interferences were Klebsiella pneumonia (10000), Salmonella enteritidis (10000), Bacilli (1000), Streptococcus agalactiae (1000) and Escherichia coli (100). Therefore, Escherichia coli cause 5% error in fluorescence of N, Cl-CDs@van/Staphylococcus aureus (102 CFU ml−1) system when its concentration reaches to 104 CFU ml−1.

All experiments were done in room temperature and physiological pH (7.4). Influence of the incubation time (0–50 min) on fluorescence of the CD@Van/S. aureus system was studied (Fig. 6 A). As shown, binding of the N, Cl-CD@van to S. aureus was completed at 30 min. Therefore, 30 min was selected as incubation time. N, Cl-CDs@van fluorescence linearly decreased with the Staphylococcus aureus concentration increase. As shown in Fig. 6B and C, there was linear behavior in the range of 102–107 CFU/mL (Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = 0.1306 CS. aureus +0.9774; R2 = 0.991). Limit of detection (LOD) was 10 CFU/mL (LOD = 3 s/m). Another experiment was done to check the behavior of N, Cl-CDs without vancomycin against bacteria. Therefore, in the similar experiment, S. aureus was added to N, Cl-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6D, fluorescence turn off (λex = 430 nm) was negligible after addition of different concentrations of S. aureus. Therefore, in the absence of vancomycin, S. aureus had no interaction with N, Cl-CDs and N, Cl-CD@Van is a probe for S. aureus rather than N, Cl-CDs.

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is that N, Cl-CDs@van must be attached to the surface of bacteria. The attachment of nano-particle@van to bacteria was confirmed by fluorescence microscopy, EDX and HRTEM imaging before and after addition of nano-particle@van [30–32]. The more attachments provide the more fluorescence quenching. Based on analytical chemistry viewpoint, detection of lower concentrations (higher sensitivity) is possible when analytical signal has significant change (more attachments). This depends to properties of doped-CDs, vancomycin and the cell wall of bacteria. N, Cl-CDs have negative charge based on zeta potential (Fig. 3B). The major constituents of the cell walls of gram-positive bacteria are peptidoglycan, teichuronic acids, (lipo)teichoic acids, (lipo)proteins, (lipo)polysaccharides [48]. The cell wall of the Gram positive bacteria have D-alanyl-d-alanine of N-acetylmuramic acid and N-acetylglucosamine peptide subunits [49]. d-alanine residues have positive charge [22]. The electrostatic attraction between d-alanine residues positive charges and negative charges of the N, Cl-CDs@van surface helps this attachment. On the other hand, vancomycin can bind to C-terminal D-Ala-D-Ala residue of the peptidoglycan via polyvalent ligand−receptor interactions [49] and forms a stable complex. A systematic study may be required to gain insights into interaction of cell wall in Gram-positive bacteria with doped-CDs with positive or negative surface charges.

New method was compared with other methods in Table 1 . Each method has some advantages and disadvantages. Conventional culture methods require 3–5 days to yield results. Some methods had lower detection limits such as fluorescence (core–shell CDs@BONs) [35], chemiluminesce (ALP-IgG, MB@Van) [25], electrochemistry (Apt-MB AuNCs@van) [26], ELISA (IgG, AuNPs, MB@Van) [27], and colorimetry (Apt, AuNPs, van) [29]. Electrochemical-based methods have rapid response, cost-effective instrumentation, capability of miniaturization, large linear range and low detection limit with respect to optical and mass-based biosensor. They are not affected by sample turbidity. Disadvantages of nucleic acid-based methods are high cost and need to confined resources, denaturation or random orientation of bio-recognition element, need to complicated and several preparation steps, need to trained personnel, and need to incubation time. SERS techniques are expensive, complex and not commonly available in all laboratories [12]. Nanoparticle-based methods have the advantage of simple and rapid analysis of large volume of samples. Preparation method in the most of sensors has several steps such as aptamer and immune-based methods [25–29]. No need to expensive and complex materials, simple synthesis method, simple and short time detection, good sensitivity (D.L. = 10 CFU ml−1), wide linear range (102–107 CFU ml−1) and comparable selectivity are advantages of this new vancomycin-based fluorescence method. In comparison with CdSe QDs [21], and N-CDs@van [30], new method had lower detection limit, wider linear range, simple synthesis and better selectivity. The core–shell CDs@BONs [35] had lower detection limit but narrow linear range.

An overview of the selectivity study in the 16 works presented in Table 1 shows that 25 types bacteria (14 Gram-positive (G+) and 11 Gram-negative (G-)) were studied and Escherichia coli was the most studied bacterium (G-, studied in 15 works) and followed by Listeria monocytogenes (G+, 7), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (G-, 6), Bacillus subtilis (G+, 6), Salmonella typhimurium (G+, 5), Bacillus cereus (G+, 3) and Salmonella enteritidis (G-, 3). Unfortunately, in the most of these works, target bacteria were studied separately with probe system in one concentration and results were compared with Staphylococcus aureus (G+) and no tolerance limits for simultaneous presence of bacteria were reported except in a few works [23, 25, 29, 31, and this work]. Some references [ [17,24–26]] have used dual systems to increase the selectivity but their studies were limited to separate interaction of bacteria with probe in one concentration. In one of these dual systems, Yang et al. [25] were studied effect of bacteria mixture on analytical signal but observed 23.1% and 57.6% decrease in response signal at 10 and 100 concentration ratios. Another result from this overview is that Gram-negative bacteria had no or little interference in vancomycin-based methods because of specific interaction of this molecule. Selectivity in these methods requires to specific binding to the wall of bacteria. Attention to the following points as future directions can be useful for better design of sensors. Characteristics of gram-positive bacteria are 1) no outer membrane, 2) complex cell wall that consists of peptidoglycan, polysaccharides, teichoic acids, and proteins, 3) thick peptidoglycan layer and 4) certain surface appendages (flagella and hair-like structures called pili). The gram-positive bacteria are further classified into cocci with circular shape (further categorized to Staphylococcus: grape-like clusters and Streptococcus: chain like) and bacilli with rod shape (further categorized to spore-forming and non-spore-forming). Significant differences are observed in the peptide/polysaccharide ratio in Gram-positive bacteria [48]. Notable differences attributed to the peptide composition, the degree of peptide crosslinks, peptidoglycan chains length and amount of surface dissociable groups such as carboxyl, phosphate, sulfate or amino groups. Vancomycin can bind to the C-terminal D-Ala-D-Ala residues. Any false binding sites lead to decrease in selectivity. Therefore, more systematic selectivity studies with considering different cell wall composition in different groups of Gram-positive bacteria and tolerance limits for interference are required.

For real sample analysis, S. aureus was determined in orange juice. First, the orange juice was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and then, various concentrations of S. aureus (104, 105, 106 and 107 CFU/mL) were added to the juice [30]. Good recoveries (96%–103.2%) were obtained (Table 2 ).

In summary, N, Cl-CDs were prepared in choline chloride-glycerol deep eutectic solvent. N, Cl-CDs were modified with vancomycin and used to detect a Gram-positive bacterium (Staphylococcus aureus). The N, Cl-CDs@van attaches to the surfaces of Staphylococcus aureus and leads to N, Cl-CDs@van fluorescence turn off. Staphylococcus aureus was determined in the range of 102–107 CFU/mL. Advantages of the new method for Staphylococcus aureus detection are wide dynamic range, simple preparation of CDs and acceptable detection limit.
Reza Tabaraki: Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Fereshteh Nazari: Writing – original draft, Carried out the experiment, wrote - original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank Dr. Hadi Hosseini, Department of Chemistry, Ilam University for XPS fitting.
